questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The cold junction of a thermocouple is at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The thermo e.m.f produced in the thermocouple is given by an equation $E=16 T-0.04 T^2$, where $T$ is the temperature of the hot junction. The temperature of inversion and neutral temperature of the thermocouple are
questions.solution:
Thermo emf $E=16 T-0.04 T^2$
At inversion temperature $\left(T i\right), E=0$
$$
\therefore \quad \begin{aligned}
0 & =16 T_i-0.04 T_i^2 \\
16 T i & =0.04 T_i^2 \\
T i & =\frac{16}{0.04}=400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$
Neutral temperature $T_n=\frac{T i+T}{2}$
$T=$ Temperature of cold end
$$
T_n=\frac{400+0}{2}=200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: In an adiabatic change, the pressure and temperature of a monoatomic gas are related with relation $p \propto T^{C}$, where $C$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Key Idea Poisson's equation for adiabatic process is given by
$$
p V^{\gamma}=\text { constant. }
$$
For adiabatic process, Poisson's equation is given by
$$
p V^{\gamma}=\text { constant }
$$
Ideal gas relation is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& p V &=R T \\
\Rightarrow & V &=\frac{R T}{p}
\end{aligned}
$$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$$
p\left(\frac{R T}{p}\right)^{\gamma}=\text { constant }
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{T^{\gamma}}{p^{\gamma-1}}=\text { constant }
$$
where $\gamma$ is ratio of specific heats of the gas
Given, $\quad p \propto T^{C} \quad$... (iv)
On comparing with Eq. (iii), we have
$$
C=\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}
$$
For a monoatomic gas $\gamma=\frac{5}{3}$
We have
$$
C=\frac{\frac{5}{3}}{\frac{5}{3}-1}=\frac{5}{2}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The cold junction of a thermocouple is at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The thermo e.m.f produced in the thermocouple is given by an equation $E=16 T-0.04 T^2$, where $T$ is the temperature of the hot junction. The temperature of inversion and neutral temperature of the thermocouple are
questions.solution:
Thermo emf $E=16 T-0.04 T^2$
At inversion temperature $\left(T i\right), E=0$
$$
\therefore \quad \begin{aligned}
0 & =16 T_i-0.04 T_i^2 \\
16 T i & =0.04 T_i^2 \\
T i & =\frac{16}{0.04}=400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}
$$
Neutral temperature $T_n=\frac{T i+T}{2}$
$T=$ Temperature of cold end
$$
T_n=\frac{400+0}{2}=200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A steel tape measures the length of a copper rod as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> when both are at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, the calibration temperature for the tape. What would be the tape reading for the length of the rod when both are at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Given, <math><mi>α</mi><mi> </mi></math>for steel<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> </mi><mn>1.2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi></math>and<math><mi> </mi><mi>α</mi><mi> </mi></math>for copper is<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi> </mi><mn>1.7</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
questions.solution:
As the steel tape is calibrated at 10 °C, therefore, adjacent centimeter marks on the steel tape will be separated by a distance of

lt=l101+ αs∆T=(1+αs20) cm

Length of copper rod at 30 °C

= 901+αc20cm

Therefore, number of centimeters read on the tape will be

= 901+αc2011+αs20= 901+1.7×10-5×2011+1.2×10-5×20

= 90×1.000341.00024=90.01 cm
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A mental sphere of radius $r$ and specific heat $S$ is rotated about an axis passing through its centre at a speed of $n$ rotations per second. It is suddenly stopped and $50 \%$ of its energy is used in increasing its temperature. Then, the raise in temperature of the sphere is
questions.solution:
Rotational KE of sphere $=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$
For sphere, $\quad I=\frac{2}{5} m r^2$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { KE } \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5} m r^2(2 \pi n)^2=\frac{1}{5} m r^2 \times 4 \pi^2 n^2 \\
& =\frac{4}{5} m r^2 \pi^2 n^2 \mathrm{~J} \\
& \therefore \text { Heat produced } \Delta Q=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KE} \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{4}{5} m r^2 \pi^2 n^2\right) \\
& =\frac{2}{5} m r^2 \pi^2 n^2 \\
& \therefore \quad \Delta Q=m s \Delta t \\
& \therefore \text { In temperature } \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t & =\frac{\Delta Q}{m s}=\frac{2}{5} \frac{m r^2 \pi^2 n^2}{m \cdot s} \\
& =\frac{2}{5} \frac{r^2 \pi^2 n^2}{s}
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two slabs $A$ and $B$ of equal surface area are placed one over the other such that their surfaces are completely in contact. The thickness of slab $A$ is twice that of $B$. The coefficient of thermal conductivity of slab $A$ is twice that of $B$. The first surface of slab $A$ is maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, while the second surface of slab $B$ is maintained at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature at the contact of their surfaces is
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
A metal rod of length $\mathrm{L}$ and cross-sectional area $\mathrm{A}$ is heated through $\mathrm{T}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the force required to prevent the expansion of the rod lengthwise?
(Y= Young's modulus of material of the rod, $\alpha=$ coefficient of linear expansion of
the rod.)
questions.solution:
The increase in length due to heating is given by
$\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L}_{0}(1+\alpha \mathrm{T}) \quad$ or $\quad \mathrm{L}-\mathrm{L}_{0}=\Delta \mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L}_{0} \alpha \mathrm{T}$
To compress the rod by $\Delta \mathrm{L}$, the force required is given by
$\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{YA} \Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}$
Substituting the value of $\mathrm{L}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{L}$ we get
$\mathrm{F}=\frac{\mathrm{YA} \times \mathrm{L}_{0} \alpha \mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{o}}(1+\alpha \mathrm{T})}=\frac{\mathrm{YA} \alpha \mathrm{T}}{1+\alpha \mathrm{T}}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A cylindrical rod has temperature ' $\mathrm{T}_1$ ' and ' $\mathrm{T}_2$ ' at its ends. The rate of flow of heat is ' $\mathrm{Q}_1$ ' $\mathrm{cal} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$. If length and radius of the rod are doubled keeping temperature constant, then the rate of flow of heat ' $Q_2$ ' will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}_1=\frac{\mathrm{kA}\left(\mathrm{T}_1-\mathrm{T}_2\right)}{\mathrm{L}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{k} \pi \mathrm{r}_1^2\left(\mathrm{~T}_1-\mathrm{T}_2\right)}{\mathrm{L}_1} \\ & \mathrm{Q}_2=\frac{\mathrm{k} \pi \mathrm{r}_2^2\left(\mathrm{~T}_1-\mathrm{T}_2\right)}{\mathrm{L}_2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{\mathrm{Q}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_2^2}{\mathrm{r}_1^2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{L}_1}{\mathrm{~L}_2}=(2)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}=2 \\ & \therefore \mathrm{Q}_2=2 \mathrm{Q}_1\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two rods of the same length and material transfer a given amount of heat in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, when they are joined end to end, i.e. in series. When they are joined in parallel, they will transfer the same heat under the same conditions in
questions.solution:
∆t=∆Q∆xKA∆T

When two rods of same length are joined in parallel,

A →2 and ∆x→12 times

∴ ∆t becomes14 times i.e.,14×12 s=3 s
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The densities of a liquid at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are respectively 1.0127 and 1 . A specific gravity bottle is filled with $300 \mathrm{~g}$ of the liquid at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ upto the brim and it is heated to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Then, the mass of the liquid expelled in grams is: (Coefficient of linear expansion of glass $\left.=9 \times 10^{-6} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$
questions.solution:
Density at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \rho_0=1.0127$
Density at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \rho_{100}=1$
Coefficient of real expansion of liquid,
$\gamma_{\text {real }}=\frac{\rho_0-\rho_{100}}{\rho_{100} \times \Delta t}$
$=\frac{1.0127-1}{1 \times 100}=0.0127 \times 10^{-2}$
$=1.27 \times 10^{-4}$
$\gamma_{\text {real }}=\gamma_{\text {app }}+\gamma_g$
$\gamma_g=$ coefficient of volume expansion of glass $=3 \alpha$
$\therefore \quad 1.27 \times 10^{-4}=\gamma_{\mathrm{app}}+3 \alpha$
$1.27 \times 10^{-4}=\gamma_{\text {app }}+3 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}$
$\gamma_{\mathrm{app}}=1.27 \times 10^{-4}-27 \times 10^{-6}$
$=1.27 \times 10^{-4}-0.27 \times 10^{-4}$
$=1 \times 10^{-4}$
$\therefore \quad \gamma_{\text {app }}=\frac{\text { mass expelled }}{\text { remaining mass } \times \Delta t}$
$1 \times 10^{-4}=\frac{m_1-m_2}{m_2 \times 100}$
$\left(\frac{m_1}{m_2}-1\right)=1 \times 10^{-4} \times 100=10^{-2}$
$\frac{m_1}{m_2}=1+10^{-2}=1.01$
$m_2=\frac{m_1}{1.01}=\frac{300}{1.01}$
Mass expelled $=m_1-m_2$
$=300-\frac{300}{1.01}$
$=\frac{3}{1.01}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A beaker of height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>H</mi></math> is made up of a material whose coefficient of linear thermal expansion is <math> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math>. It is filled up to the brim by a liquid whose coefficient of volume expansion is <math> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> <mi>α</mi> </mrow> </math>. If now the beaker along with its contents is uniformly heated through a small temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>, the level of liquid will reduce by (Given, <math> <mrow> <mi>α</mi><mi>T</mi><mo>≪</mo><mn>1</mn> </mrow> </math>)
questions.solution:
Let A is cross - sectional area of a beaker

Then, final cross - sectional area

               A ′ = A 1 + 6 α T

Final volume of liquid

              V ′ = AH 1 + 3 α T

Final height,     H ′ = V ′ A ′

⇒           H ′ = H 1 + 3 α T 1 + 6 α T ∼ H 1 - 3 α T

Reduction in level,   H - H ′ = 3 α TH
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A conducting rod of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ has area of cross-section $10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$. One end is immersed in boiling water $\left(100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ and the other end in Ice $\left(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. If coefficient of thermal conductivity of rod is $96 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s} . \mathrm{m}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and latent heat for ice is $8 \times 10^4 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{kg}$ then the amount of ice which will melt in one minute is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\frac{\mathrm{KA}(\Delta \theta) \mathrm{t}}{\ell}=\mathrm{mL} \\ & \mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{KA}(\Delta \theta) \mathrm{t}}{\ell \mathrm{L}}=\frac{96 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 60}{1 \times 8 \times 10^4} \\ & =7.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Ice starts forming in a lake where the water is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and the ambient temperature is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. If the time taken for <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mn>c</mn><mn>m</mn></math> of ice to be formed is <math><mi>7 hours</mi></math>, then the time taken for the thickness of ice to change from <math><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mn>c</mn><mn>m</mn></math> to <math><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mn>c</mn><mn>m</mn></math> is
questions.solution:
If at some time t, the thickness of ice is x and at time t+dt, the thickeness is x+dx, then

ρAdxLfusion=kA0--10xdt

⇒∆t=ρLfusion20kx22-x12

⇒∆t∝(x22-x12)

⇒∆t∆t'=x22-x12x'22-x'12

⇒7∆t'=(12-02)(22-12)

⇒∆t'=21 hours
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: What is change in volume of an iron sphere of volume $500 \mathrm{~cm}^3$, when it is heated from $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $\left(\alpha_{\text {Iron }}=12 \times 10^{-6 /{ }^{\circ}} \mathrm{C}\right)$ ?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\text { Change in Vol. }}{\text { Original Vol. }}=\text { Coe. of vol. expansion } \times \text { temp change } \\ & \Rightarrow \Delta V=V \times\left(3 \alpha_{\text {Iron }}\right) \times(\Delta T) \\ & \Rightarrow \Delta V=500 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \times\left[3 \times\left(12 \times 10^{-6 /{ }^{\circ}} \mathrm{C}\right)\right] \times\left(100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right) \\ & \Delta V=500 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~cm}^3=1.8 \mathrm{~cm}^3\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A <math> <mrow> <mtext>1 L</mtext></mrow> </math> glass flask contains some mercury. It is found that at different temperatures the volume of air inside the flask remains the same. What is the volume of mercury in this flask if coefficient of linear expansion of glass is <math> <mrow> <mtext>9</mtext><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mtext>10</mtext></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow> </msup> <mo>/</mo><mmultiscripts> <mtext>C</mtext> <mprescripts></mprescripts> <none></none> <mo>∘</mo> </mmultiscripts> </mrow> </math> while of volume expansion of mercury is <math> <mrow> <mtext>1</mtext><mtext>.8</mtext><mo>×</mo><msup> <mrow> <mtext>10</mtext></mrow> <mrow> <mo>−</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow> </msup> <mo>/</mo><mmultiscripts> <mtext>C</mtext> <mprescripts></mprescripts> <none></none> <mtext>o</mtext> </mmultiscripts> </mrow> </math>?
questions.solution:
It is given that the volume of air in the flask remains the same. This means that the expansion in the volume of the vessel is exactly equal to the volume expansion of mercury.

i.e.,            Δ V G = Δ V L

or        V G γ G Δ θ = V L γ L Δ θ

∴     V L = V G γ G γ L = 1 0 0 0 × 3 × 9 × 1 0 - 6 1.8 × 1 0 - 4 = 1 5 0 cc
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: When a liquid in a glass vessel is heated, its apparent expansion coefficient is 10.30×10-4 ℃-1. When the same liquid is heated in a metal vessel, its apparent expansion coefficient is 10.06×10-4 ℃-1. If the coefficient of linear expansion of glass = 9×10-6 ℃-1, what is the coefficient of linear expansion of metal?
questions.solution:
Here, γag=10.30 ×10-4 ℃-1

γam=10.06×10-4  ℃-1

αg=9×10-6 ℃-1, αm= ?

Now, γr= γag+γg= γam+γm

∴10.30×10-4+3×9×10-6=10.06×10-4+γm      [∵γg=3×αa]

∴ γm=10.30+0.27-10.0610-4=0.51×10-4

αm=13γm= 0.51×10-43

= 0.17×10-4=17×10-6 ℃-1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A bimetallic strip is formed out of two identical strips, one of copper and the other of brass. The coefficients of linear expansion of the two metals are $\alpha_C$ and $\alpha_B$. On heating, the temperature of the strip increases by $\Delta T$ and the strip bends to form an arc of radius $R$. Then $R$ is proportional to
questions.solution:
Let $L_0$ be the initial length of each strip before heating. Length after heating will be
$L_B=L_0\left(1+\alpha_B \Delta T\right)$ $=(R+d) \theta$ $L_C=L_0\left(1+\alpha_C \Delta T\right)=R \theta$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{R+d}{R}=\frac{1+\alpha_B \Delta T}{1+\alpha_C \Delta T}$ $1+\frac{d}{R}=1+\left(\alpha_B-\alpha_C\right) \Delta T$ $R=\frac{d}{\left(\alpha_B-\alpha_C\right) \Delta T}$ $R \propto \frac{1}{\Delta T}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A steam at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is passed into $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water contained in a calorimeter of water equivalent $0.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ at $9^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ till the temperature of the calorimeter and water in it is increased to $90^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The mass of steam condensed in $\mathrm{kg}$ is nearly (specific heat of water $=1 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, latent heat of vaporisation $=540 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}$ )
questions.solution:
By calorimetry,
Heat lost $=$ Heat gained
$$
\begin{aligned}
m \times L+ & m \times C \times\left(T_1-T_2\right) \\
& =\left(m_1+m_2\right) \times C \times\left(T_2-T_3\right) \\
m \times 540 & +m \times 1 \times(100-90) \\
& =(1000 \mathrm{~g}+200 \mathrm{~g}) \times 1 \times(90-9) \\
& =m \times 540+10 m=1200 \times 81 \\
550 m & =1200 \times 81 \\
m & =\frac{1200 \times 81}{550}=\frac{24 \times 81}{11} \mathrm{~g} \\
& =\frac{1944}{11} \mathrm{~g}=176 \mathrm{~g}=0.176 \mathrm{~kg}
\end{aligned}
$$
or $m=0.18 \mathrm{~kg}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Three rods $A B, B C$ and $B D$ made of the same material and having the same cross-section have been joined as shown in the figure.
The ends $A, C$ and $D$ are held at temperatures of $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. If each rod is of same length, then the temperature at the junction $B$ of the three rods is
questions.solution:
Let the temperature of function $\theta$. Since, $\operatorname{rod} B C$ and $B D$ are parallel to each other (because both having the same temperature difference). Hence, given figure can be redrawn as follows ( $R$ be the resistance of each rod).
$\because \frac{Q}{t}=\frac{\left(\theta_1-\theta_2\right)}{R}$ and $\left(\frac{Q}{t}\right)_{A B}=\left(\frac{Q}{t}\right)_{B D}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{(80-\theta)}{R / 2}=\frac{\left(\theta-20^{\circ}\right)}{R} \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A hunter fired a metallic bullet of mass $m \mathrm{~kg}$ from a gun towards an obstacle and it just melts when it is stopped by the obstacle. The initial temperature of the bullet is $300 \mathrm{~K}$. If $\frac{1}{4}$ th of heat is absorbed by the obstacle, then the minimum velocity of the bullet is (Melting point of bullet $=600 \mathrm{~K}$, Specific heat of bullet $=0.03 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{g}^{-1}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$, Latent heat of fusion of bullet $=6 \mathrm{cal} \mathrm{g}^{-1}$ )
questions.solution:
As per question $\frac{1}{4}$ th of heat is absorbed by the obstacle so, $\frac{3}{4}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^2\right)=m s \Delta \theta+m L$
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{3}{4} v^2=0.03 \times 4200 \times 300+6 \times 4200 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v^2=\frac{8}{3}[0.01 \times 4200 \times 300+2 \times 4200] \\
& \Rightarrow v^2=40 \times 4200 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v=\sqrt{168000}=410 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Two metal slabs of same cross-sectional area have thicknesses $d_1$ and ; and thermal conductivities $K_1$ and $K_2$ respectively, are connected in series. The free ends of the two slabs are kept at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2\left(T_1>T_2\right)$. The temperature $T$ of their common junction is
questions.solution:
Heat current, $\dot{Q}_1=\frac{K_1\left(T_1-T\right) A}{d_1}$
For second slab,
Heat current, $\dot{Q}_2=\frac{K_2\left(T-T_2\right) A}{d_2}$
As slabs are in series same heat current flows through them
$\dot{Q}_1=\dot{Q}_2$
$\therefore \frac{K_1\left(T_1-T\right) A}{d_1}=\frac{K_2\left(T-T_2\right) A}{d_2}$
Therefore, temperature $T$ of their common junction is
$T=\frac{K_1 T_1 d_2+K_2 T_2 d_1}{K_2 d_1+K_1 d_2}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The coefficient of volume expansion of glycerine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The fractional change in its density for a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> rise in temperature will be
questions.solution:
Let V0 be the initial volume of glycerine, i.e, at 0 °C (dry). If Vt be its volume at 30 °C

Then Vt=V01+v∆t

=  V01+49×10-5×30 

Vt=V01+0.01470=1.0147070 V0

⇒     V0Vt=11.01470

Let ρo and ρt be the initial and final densities of glycertine then initial density, ρ0=mv0 and final density, ρt=mVt

Where, m=mass of glycertine

∆ρρ0= fractional change in the density

ρt-ρ0ρ0=m1Vt-1V0mV0=V0Vt-1

∆ρρ0=11.01470-1=-0.0145

Here, negative sign shows that density decreases with rise in temperature

∆ρρ0=0.0145=1.45×10-2

∆ρρ0=1.45×10-2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A seconds pendulum clock having steel wire is calibrated at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. When temperature is increased to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, then calculate how much time does the clock lose or gain in one week <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>α</mi><mtext>steel</mtext></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mtext>.</mtext><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mn>0</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mspace></mspace><mo>∘</mo></msup><msup><mtext>C</mtext><mn>-1</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
questions.solution:
The time period of the second's pendulum = 2 s

Change in the time period, Δ T = 1 2 T α Δ θ

Δ T = 1 2   2   1 . 2 × 1 0 - 5   3 0 ∘ - 2 0 ∘

       = 1 . 2 × 1 0 - 4  s

New time period,  T ′ = T + Δ T

                              T ′ = 2 . 0 0 0 1 2  s

Time lost in a week,

                    Δ t = Δ T T ′ × t

                         = 1 . 2 × 1 0 - 4 2 . 0 0 0 1 2 × 7 × 2 4 × 3 6 0 0

                        = 3 6 . 28 s
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Three rods of material x and three rods of material y of identical length and cross sectional area are connected as shown in the figure. If the end A is maintained at 60∘C and the junction E at 10 ∘ C , then the temperature of junction C is [Given the thermal conductivity of x is 0.92 W/m.K and that of y  is 0.46 W/m.K]
questions.solution:
Treating the given network of rods in terms of thermal resistance Rx and Ry with

R x = L A × 0.92 and R y = L A × 0.46 as R = L AK

so that if   Rx = R,  Ry = 2Rx = 2R

Now as in this bridge [(P/Q) = (R/S)], so the bridge is balanced, i.e., the temperature at junctions C and D is equal and the rod CD becomes ineffective as no heat will flow through it.

Now as the thermal resistance of the bridge between junctions B and E is

1 R BE = 1 R + R + 1 2R + 2R , i.e., R BE = 4 3 R

The total resistance of bridge between A and E will be

      Req = RAB + RBE = 2R + (4/3) R = (10/3) R

So the net rate of flow of heat through the bridge will be

dQ dt = Δ θ R eq = 60 - 10 10/3 R = 15 R

Now if TB is the temperature at B,

[ dQ dt ] AB = Δ θ R AB = 60 - T B 2 R

But [ dQ dt ] AB = dQ dt , i.e., 60 - T B 2 R = 15 R , i.e., T B = 30 o C

Also at B

[ dQ dt ] AB = [ dQ dt ] BC + [ dQ dt ] BD

i.e., 15 R = 30 - T C R + 30 - T D 2R

and as     TC = TD = T,    30 = 3 (30 - T),

i.e.,          TC = TD = T = 20o C
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The coefficient of linear expansion of brass and steel rods are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ respectively. Lengths of brass and steel rods are $l_1$ and $l_2$ respectively. If $\left(l_2-l_1\right)$ is maintained same at all temperatures, which one of the following relations is correct?
questions.solution:
The correct option is (D).
Concept: Length of a metalic rod at a given temperature is given by,
$l=l_0(1+\alpha \Delta T)$
where, $l_0$ is the initial length, $\alpha$ the coefficient of linear expansion and $\Delta T$ the temperature change.
At any given temperature the lengths of the brass and steel rods can be written as, $l_b=l_1\left(1+\alpha_1 \Delta T\right)$ and $l_s=l_2\left(1+\alpha_2 \Delta T\right)$ respectively.
Therefore, at difference of length is given by,
$\begin{aligned} & l_b-l_s=l_1\left(1+\alpha_1 \Delta T\right)-l_2\left(1+\alpha_2 \Delta T\right) \\ & \Rightarrow l_b-l_s=\left(l_1-l_2\right)+\left(\alpha_1 l_1-l_2 \alpha_2\right) \Delta T\end{aligned}$
Since, $l_b-l_{\mathrm{s}}=l_1-l_2$ as the difference of length remains constant.
Therefore, $\left(\alpha_1 l_1-l_2 \alpha_2\right)=0$
So, $\alpha_1 l_1=l_2 \alpha_2$ is the answer.
Since, $l_b-l_s=l_1-l_2$ as the difference of length remains constant.
Therefore, $\left(\alpha_1 l_1-l_2 \alpha_2\right)=0$ So, $\alpha_1 l_1=l_2 \alpha_2$ is the answer.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics

questions.q: A wall is made of equally thick layers $P$ and $Q$ of different materials. Thermal conductivity of $Q$ is half of that of the $P$. In the steady state, if the temperature difference across the wall is $24^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the temperature difference across the layer ' $P$ ' is ...............  .
questions.solution:
We have, $$ \begin{array}{ll} K_Q=\frac{K_P}{2} \Rightarrow K_P=2 K_Q & \frac{2 K_Q a\left(T_1-T_0\right)}{x}=\frac{K_Q a\left(T_0-T_2\right)}{x} \\ \frac{}{x} & {\left[\because\left[\frac{Q}{t}\right]_P=\left[\frac{Q}{t}\right]_Q\right]} \\ 2\left(T_1-T_0\right)=T_0-T_2 & \end{array} $$
According to the question,
Now, from Eq. (i), $$ \begin{aligned} & 2 T_1-3 T_0+T_1-24=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3 T_1-3 T_0=24 \Rightarrow T_1-T_0=8^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A partition wall has two layers of different materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> in contact with each other. They have the same thickness but the thermal conductivity of layer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is twice that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. At steady state, if the temperature difference across the layer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>, then the corresponding temperature difference across the layer <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Let T be the junction temperature

Here, KA=2KB, T-TB=50K

At the steady state HA=HB

⇒      K A A T A −T L 2 = K B A  T− T B L 2

⇒  2KB TA-T=KB T-TB

⇒     TA-T=T-TB2=502=25 K
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Physics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A copper block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is heated in furnace to a temperature of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo>°</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and then placed on a large ice block. What is the maximum amount of ice that can melt ? (Specific heat of copper is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>; heat of fusion of water is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>335</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
questions.solution:
Given, mass of copper block m=2.5 kg

Change in temperature Δt=500°C

Specific heat s=0.39 J (g-K)-1
 
                          =390 J (kg-K)-1

Latent heat of fusion of water L=335 J g-1
                                                     = 335×103 J kg-1

Heat energy absorbed by copper block

                                                   Q1=msΔt

                                                          =2.5×390×500 J

Let m' kg of ice be melted.

Heat energy required to melt ice, Q2=m'L

But heat energy absorbed by copper block is  Heat energy utilised by ice in melting

                Q1=Q2

        msΔt=m'L

or           m'=ms ΔtL

                    = 2 . 5 × 3 9 0 × 5 0 0 3 3 5 × 1 0 3

                     = 1 . 4 5 5  kg

                       ≈ 1 . 5  kg
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
